coredata.dc:title: Study of ethosuximide detection using a novel molecularly imprinted electrochemiluminescence sensor based on tris(2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium(II)@nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiao-Lei
Qi, Ji-Lan
Feng, Fang
Yang, Gong-Jun
coredata.dc:description: A novel molecularly imprinted electrochemiluminescene (ECL) sensor was fabricated for the detection of ethosuximide. The new Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs (tris(2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium(II)@nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots) nanocomposite was synthesized by electrostatic interaction between Ru(pby)3 2+ and N-GQDs, which showed excellent and stable ECL properties. And then dopamine as functional monomer and ethosuximide as template molecule were co-polymerized by hydrogen bonding on the surface of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion. After ethosuximide was extracted with H2SO4, the molecularly imprinted cavities in film were formed. When ethosuximide rebound onto imprinted cavities again, the ECL intensity of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs decreased. Based on this principle, the quantitative relationship between the concentration of ethosuximide (c) and the variation of ECL intensity (ΔI ECL) was established. Namely the ΔI ECL depended linearly on the log c in the range from 5.00 × 10−7 to 1.00 × 10−4 mol/L with a detection limit of 3.00 × 10−7 mol/L (S/N = 3), and the correlation coefficient was 0.9984. The proposed sensor was also applied in plasma and urine samples with recovery in range from 97.80% to 106.2% (RSD < 6.3%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs
Electrochemiluminescene sensor
Molecularly imprinted
Ethosuximide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the MIP ECL sensor
Detection of ESM with the MIP ECL sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs (A) and insert of (B), EDS spectrum of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs(B), SEM of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE (C), PDA-ESM/Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE (D) and MIP/Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE (E).

(A) ECL-potential curves of different modified electrodes. (a) PDA-ESM/Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE, (b) MIP/Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE after incubating with ESM, (c) MIP/Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE, (d) Ru(pby)3 2+/Nafion/GCE and (e) Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE. Working buffer: 0.1 mol/L PBS (pH 7.4) containing 20 mmol/L TEA; The applied potential: 0.2–1.0 V; Scan rate: 100 mV/s; The voltage of the photomultiplier tube: 800 V. (B) Nyquist plot for Faradaic impedance measurements in 0.1 mol/L PBS (pH 7.4) and 0.1 mol/L KNO3 containing 5.0 mmol/L K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] at: (a) bare GCE, (b) Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE, (c) PDA-ESM/Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE, (d) MIP/Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE and (e) Ru(pby)3 2+/Nafion/GCE.
Effect of ratio of Ru(pby)3 2+ to N-GQDs in composite (A), pH (B) and electropolymerization cycles (C) on ECL intensity.

(A) The ECL stability of proposed sensor with addition of various concentrations of ESM. a ~ g: 0.00, 5.00 × 10−7, 1.00 × 10−6, 3.00 × 10−6, 1.00 × 10−5, 4.00 × 10−5 and 1.00 × 10−4 mol/L, respectively. (B) Calibration curve for ESM determination.
The selectivity of the proposed sensor by compared with different interfering substances.
The schematic diagram of preparing MIP ECL sensor.
Comparison of the ESM detection with other analysis methods.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Abbreviation: GC–MS: Gas chromatographic mass spectrometry; MEKC: Micellar electrokinetic capillary chromatography; UPLC–MS/MS: Ultra performance liquid chromatography tandemmass spectrometry.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recoveries of ESM in plasma and urine samples.

head.ce:title: Study of ethosuximide detection using a novel molecularly imprinted electrochemiluminescence sensor based on tris(2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium(II)@nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Drug Quality Control and Pharmacovigilance (China Pharmaceutical University), Ministry of Education, School of Pharmacy, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Drug Quality Control and Pharmacovigilance (China Pharmaceutical University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Drug Quality Control and Pharmacovigilance (China Pharmaceutical University), Ministry of Education, School of Pharmacy, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Pharmacy, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel molecularly imprinted electrochemiluminescene (ECL) sensor was fabricated for the detection of ethosuximide. The new Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs (tris(2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium(II)@nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots) nanocomposite was synthesized by electrostatic interaction between Ru(pby)3 2+ and N-GQDs, which showed excellent and stable ECL properties. And then dopamine as functional monomer and ethosuximide as template molecule were co-polymerized by hydrogen bonding on the surface of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion. After ethosuximide was extracted with H2SO4, the molecularly imprinted cavities in film were formed. When ethosuximide rebound onto imprinted cavities again, the ECL intensity of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs decreased. Based on this principle, the quantitative relationship between the concentration of ethosuximide (c) and the variation of ECL intensity (ΔI ECL) was established. Namely the ΔI ECL depended linearly on the log c in the range from 5.00 × 10−7 to 1.00 × 10−4 mol/L with a detection limit of 3.00 × 10−7 mol/L (S/N = 3), and the correlation coefficient was 0.9984. The proposed sensor was also applied in plasma and urine samples with recovery in range from 97.80% to 106.2% (RSD < 6.3%).

The schematic diagram of preparing MIP ECL sensor.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Ethosuximide (3-ethyl-3-methylpyrrolidine-2,5-dione, ESM) is an antiepileptic drug and it is widely used for treatment of absence seizures which require long term medication [1–3]. Accurate measurement of drug in body fluid is extremely helpful for the optimization of therapy with drugs as well as prevention of the toxicity from overdosage and severe side effects, especially in the infant and child [4]. At present, the detection methods commonly used for ESM include gas chromatography (GC) by chiral method [5], chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [6], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) by derivatization [7,8], ultra performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS) [9,10] and micellar electrokinetic chromatography (MEKC) [11]. However, the sample pre-process of these methods is more complicated and cumbersome, and detection is time consuming. Thus, it is important to establish a simple and convenient detection method for selective determination of ethosuximide.

Electrochemiluminescence is chemiluminescence triggered by electrochemical techniques [12]. In addition to some characteristics of fluorescence analysis and chemiluminescence analysis, ECL also performs electrochemical characteristics. Therefore, the main characteristics and advantages of ECL are its controllable reaction time and space, its high sensitivity and specificity, and its minimal hardware requirements [13]. Thus it becomes an ideal analytical detection method for pharmaceutical research, clinical diagnostics and environmental monitoring. The Ru(bpy)3 2+ is the most common ECL luminescent reagent with very broad applications due to its good stability and ECL efficiency in aqueous media [14]. In the traditional method for ECL analysis in solution phase, Ru(bpy)3 2+ must be continuously added to the detection pool. This not only wastes expensive reagents, but also complicates the detection device. By immobilizing Ru(bpy)3 2+ on an electrode surface, solid-state ECL could overcome the above mentioned difficulties and provide several advantages over solution-phase ECL [15]. Therefore, various methods have been tried, and it is expected that the Ru(bpy)3 2+ can be fixed to the electrode surface more and more stably. The electrostatic interaction between the positively charged Ru(bpy)3 2+ and the negatively charged Nafion membrane makes them stable on the electrode surface. So Nafion is widely used as supporting substance in immobilizing Ru(bpy)3 2+ on the electrode surface [16]. However, the Nafion membrane is relatively dense, and the Ru(bpy)3 2+ in the membrane easily enters the non-electroactive hydrophobic region of the membrane, partially losing activity, resulting in decreased stability. Nanomaterials, which have large specific surface area and good electrical conductivity, are widely used in the construction of solid phase ECL sensors [17,18]. The carbon nanotubes are doped into the Nafion membrane to participate in the immobilization, which can greatly improve the electrochemical reaction current and the ECL signal [19]. There is also a combination of Nafion and graphene to support Ru(bpy)3 2+. The introduction of graphene with good conductivity not only promotes electron transfer, but also inhibits the diffusion of Ru(bpy)3 2+ into the hydrophobic non-electroactive region in the Nafion membrane, thereby maintaining better long-term stability [20]. Doping carbon nanomaterials with heteroatoms can effectively tune their intrinsic properties, including electronic characteristics, surface and local chemical features. Considering the quantum-confinement and edge effects, doping GQDs with chemically bonded N atoms can modulate the band gap of GQDs and tune electronic characteristics, local chemical properties and optical properties. Some researcher demonstrated that N-GQDs acted as advanced support materials due to their large specific surface area and excellent electrocatalytic properties [21–23]. In addition, the N-containing structure makes N-GQDs possess strong proton adsorption ability. On account of these characteristics, Ru(pby)3 2+ would be adsorbed on the N-GQDs more strongly, improving its stability and activity in Nafion membrane.

Molecular imprinting technique (MIP) is known as a technique for creation of tailor-made binding sites with memory of the shape, size and functional groups of the template molecules, and they show favored affinity to the template molecule compared to other molecules. Such techniques have been widely applied in many fields, such as solid-phase extraction, chemical sensors and artificial antibodies owing to their desired selectivity, physical robustness, thermal stability, as well as low cost and easy preparation [24–27]. Recently, polydopamine (PDA) has been used successfully for molecular imprinting due to the high stability, hydrophilicity and biocompatibility [28]. Another valuable feature of PDA lies in its chemical structure that incorporates many functional groups such as catechol, amine, and imine for the formation of noncovalent and covalent bond [29–31]. For instance, Ju et al. prepared a novel nanowire by chemical polymerization of dopamine (DA) in neutral pH (physiological condition), on which both bovine and human Hb-IPWs were imprinted [32]. The PDA nanowires with “footprints” of the template protein on the surface could reversibly rebind template protein molecules for protein specific recognition. Imprinted thin PDA film was also polymerized on the surface of silica-modified nanosized Fe3O4 magnetite for recognition and separation of bovine hemoglobin [33]. Chen et al. demonstrated that the epitope-imprinting PDA film can not only recognize the template but also recognize the corresponding protein [34].

In this study, Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs nanocomposite was prepared through the electrostatic interaction between the Ru(pby)3 2+ and N-GQDs. The prepared Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs nanocomposite was dispersed in the Nafion to form uniform Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion membrane owing to fine water solubility of N-GQDs. Furthermore, the Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion membrane also displayed better stability and ECL activity compared to Ru(pby)3 2+/Nafion membrane due to good conductivity of N-GQDs, as well as firm fixing of Ru(pby)3 2+ on N-GQDs. Then, DA used as function monomers and ESM used as template molecule were co-polymerized on the surface of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion membrane. It was found that ECL intensity could be quenched by copolymer and it would restore due to molecular imprinting cavities left after removing ESM. And when ESM rebound to cavity sites again, ECL intensity decreased with the increasing of ESM concentration. Base on this “signal-on-off” phenomenon, the molecularly imprinted ECL sensor was prepared for determination of ESM with good selectivity and sensitivity.

Dopamine hydrochloride (DA) (98.0%) was obtained from Aladdin. Tris(2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium (II) chloride hexahydrate (Ru(bpy)3 2+Cl2•6H2O) (>98.0%), and ethosuximide (ESM) (>98.0%) were purchased from Tokyo chemical industry Co., Ltd. Ammonium citrate trobasic (AC) (AR) was obtained from Shanghai trial four hervey chemical Co., Ltd. Nafion (119 solution) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Triethylamine (TEA) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All other reagents were analytically pure and used without further purification. Ultrapure water was used for the experiments.

The morphologies of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were obtained on a Model S-4800 field emission scanning electron microscope (Hitachi High-Technologies Co., Japan). The high resolution transmission electronic microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken using a Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin TEM (FEI, USA). Electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) was recorded with a CHI 660E workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instruments, China). The ECL signal was detected with a MPI-E Electrochemiluminescence Analyzer (Xi'an Remax Analyse Instrument Co., Ltd.). The conventional three-electrode configuration was used in ECL detection, including a modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter) as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode.

According to the previous literature [35], preparation of N-GQDs was as follows: 0.5 g AC and 15 mL H2O were put into a glass flak and heated to 200 °C, and a reflux lasted for 30 min until the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to orange, then the pH of obtained aqueous solution was adjusted to 7.0 by the addition of NaOH solution (10 mg/mL). By referring to a literature [36] with a slight change, the immobilization of Ru(pby)3 2+ onto N-GQDs was achieved by simply stirring a solution of Ru(pby)3 2+ (0.4 mg/mL) together with N-GQDs aqueous solution at room temperature for about 12 h, and the obtained Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The schematic diagram of preparing MIP ECL sensor was shown in Scheme 1 . Firstly, 40 μL Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs was dispersed in 10 μL Nafion solution (5.0%) and mixed homogeneously [37]. Then 5 μL of the mixed solution was carefully dropped on the surface of the well-polished bare GCE, and the solvent was allowed to evaporate at room temperature in the air. Thus Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs and Nafion modified GCE (Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE) was obtained.

Secondly, Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE was dipped in 0.01 mol/L phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) containing 5 mmol/L DA and 5 mmol/ L ESM bubbled with pure N2 (99.9%) for 10 min. The polydopamine and ESM (PDA-ESM) film on the surface of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE was prepared by performing cyclic voltammetry for 30 cycles in the range from −0.5 to 0.5 V at the scan rate of 20 mV/s [38]. Thus, PDA-ESM/Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE was obtained.

Finally, the template molecules ESM were removed from the PDA-ESM/Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE by washing continuously with 5 mmol/L H2SO4, and then MIP/Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE was successfully prepared.

In particular, because N-containing structure makes it possess strong proton (H+) adsorption ability [39], the Ru(pby)3 2+ was more tightly immobilized on N-GQDs through electrostatic adsorption to form Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs. The morphology of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs was investigated by HRTEM (shown in Fig. 1A and insert of Fig. 1B). It was clearly observed that particle size of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs was about 5 nm (Fig. 1A), and the lattic fringe had a discerned lattic spacing of 0.20 nm (insert of Fig. 1B), which matched the results from high crystallinity of the N-GQDs [35]. Besides, the EDS spectrum of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs (Fig. 1B) also demonstrated that there were C, N and Ru elements in the composite.

The SEM images of three modified electrodes surface, Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE, PDA-ESM/Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE and MIP/Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE were shown in Fig. 1C, D, E. In weak alkaline aqueous medium, DA can be polymerized by electropolymerization technique to form a continuous and smooth PDA film coating on the substrate surface [40]. And the basis of molecular recognition of MIP membrane is based on intermolecular non-covalent interactions [41,42], including static electricity, hydrogen bonding, π-π conjugate and metal chelation, and so on. Therefore, DA and ESM can co-polymerize to a thin film on the surface of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE through forming a hydrogen bond of the hydroxyl group and the amino group on the DA with the carbonyl group and the amino group on the ESM (as shown in Fig. 1D). After washing with H2SO4, the hydrogen bonds were destroyed between ESM and DA, resulting in removing of ESM to form more rough and porous MIP membrane on the surface of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE (Fig. 1E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the MIP ECL sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The fabrication process of the MIP ECL sensor was characterized by ECL in 0.1 mol/L PBS (pH 7.4) containing 20 mmol/L TEA. As shown in Fig. 2A, the Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE (Fig. 2A, curve e) produced slightly stronger ECL than Ru(pby)3 2+/Nafion/GCE (Fig. 2A, curve d). The reason may be that N-GQDs have good electrical conductivity which promotes the transmission of electrons in ECL process of Ru(pby)3 2+. The possibility of ECL mechanism is that both Ru(bpy)3 2+@N-GQDs and TEA are oxidized at the electrode surface, and Ru(bpy)3 3+@N-GQDs is reduced by TEA• to produce the excited state (see the scheme below) [43].
(1)
Ru
bpy
3
2 +
@ N − GQDs −
e −
→ Ru
bpy
3
3 +
@ N − GQDs
(2)
TEA −
e −
→
TEA
• +
(3)
OH +
TEA
• +
→
TEA •
+
H 2
O
(4)
Ru
bpy
3
3 +
@ N − GQDs +
TEA •
→ Ru
bpy
3
2 +
@ N − GQDs * + products
(5)
Ru
bpy
3
2 +
@ N − GQDs * → Ru
bpy
3
2 +
@ N − GQDs + hv

The ECL intensity were quenched (Fig. 2A, curve a) when DA and ESM were co-electropolymerized on the surface of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE, because the polymer film hindered the contact and electron transfer of co-reactants TEA with Ru(pby)3 2+ in ECL reaction. After extraction of ESM with H2SO4, the ECL intensity of MIP electrode recovered (Fig. 2A, curve c) due to TEA can diffuse into the surface of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs through imprinted cavities in polymer film. However, the ECL intensity was still lower than that of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE, because the number of imprinted cavities in the MIP film is limited, the membrane would yet hamper the diffusion of TEA toward the surface of electrode.

In addition, because EIS can reflect the interface properties of surface modified electrode, it was also employed to confirm the fabrication process of the MIP ECL sensor. As shown in Fig. 2B, the curve a presented a small semicircle domain implying low electrochemical impedance on the bare GCE. When Ru(pby)3 2+/Nafion was put onto the electrode surface, the electron transfer resistance increased significantly (Fig. 2B, curve e), the reason may be the non-conductive properties of Nafion film [44]. However, the EIS on Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE decreased apparently (Fig. 2B, curve b) because good electrical conductivity of N-GQDs lowered the electron transfer resistance. After being covered with a layer of polymeric film, the EIS on PDA-ESM/Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion/GCE (Fig. 2B, curve c) obviously increased again due to obstruction of electron transfer by non-conductive polymeric film. Finally, when the ESM was removed, the impendance on MIP electrode increased further (Fig. 2B, curve d), perhaps because film surface became more roughness. Thus, the data indicated the successful construction of the MIP ECL sensor.

To improve the performance of the MIP ECL sensor for detection of ESM, three experimental conditions, such as content ratio of Ru(pby)3 2+ to N-GQDs in nanocomposite, pH of medium and electropolymerization cycles, were optimized.

The effect of content ratio of Ru(pby)3 2+ to N-GQDs on ECL intensity was studied (Fig. 3A). When the amount of Ru(pby)3 2+ was constant, the amount of N-GQDs increased proportionally within the range of 1:1 to 1:5. It showed that ECL increased gradually with the increase proportion of N-GQDs from 1:1 to 1:4, and then decreased at 1:5. Although N-GQDs can promote ECL intensity benefiting from its good electrical conductivity, excessive N-GQDs content would possible lead to a decrease of the Ru(pby)3 2+ content immobilized on per unit volume of N-GQDs, resulting in weakening of ECL intensity. Thus Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs nanocomposite in a ratio of 1 to 4 was chosen as for further experiments because of the maximum ECL intensity obtained.

The effect of pH on ECL intensity of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs was also investigated in 0.1 mol/L PBS buffer containing 20 mmol/L TEA with different pH values ranged from 5.5 to 9.0 (Fig. 3B). The ECL intensity increased slowly with the increase of pH from 5.5 to 7.0, thereafter it increased significantly from 7.0 to 9.0. It implied that weak acidic or neutral medium was not suitable for the ECL producing process of this system. According to ECL mechanism as shown in the third and forth formulas, the electrochemical oxidation product (TEA•+) can deprotonate to form a very reductive radical (TEA•) to reduce the luminescent species. Because the deprotonation process is easier to perform in alkaline solutions, the ECL intensity increases with increasing pH of electrolyte. The basic medium may be more beneficial for the deprotonation reaction and the stability of the intermediate state in process of producing ECL with TEA as coreactant [43]. Considering detecting ESM in the pH condition of plasma, pH values of 7.4 was chosen as the optimal pH condition.

The influence of electropolymerization cycles on the polydopamine-ethosuximide film was further researched, including 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 cycles, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3C, if the number of cycles is 10 or 20, ECL intensity is lower and standard deviation is great. It is because that sparse polymer film prepared with little cycles cannot quench signal completely attributed to incompletely covered electrode surface, and it is easily damaged during process of template molecules extraction. All these factors lead to signal instability. However, if there are too many cycles, such as 40 or 50 cycles, the polymer film is too dense, and template molecules are not easily extracted, resulting in weak ECL signal. Considering the stability and intensity of signal, 30 cycles of electropolymer film is more suitable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ESM with the MIP ECL sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Under optimal experimental conditions, when the prepared ECL MIP electrode was incubated with ESM, the ECL intensity decreased (Fig. 2A, curve b) due to the ESM molecules block cavities again after rebinding to the binding sites (as shown in Scheme 1). It was also found that the ECL intensity decreased with the increasing concentration of ESM, and a relative stable curve at every concentration could be observed in Fig. 4A. So, the Fig. 4B revealed the calibration graph that ΔI ECL (where ΔI ECL was the difference value in ECL intensity before and after the addition of ESM) versus the logarithm of the ESM concentration was linearly from 5.00 × 10−7 to 1.00 × 10−4 mol/L with a detection limit of 3.00 × 10−7 mol/L (S/N = 3). The linear equation was ΔI
ECL = 2.41 × 103 lg c + 1.56 × 104 (where c was the concentration of ESM), and the correlation coefficient was 0.9984. It showed that this new detection method using ECL MIP sensor performed a more wide linear range and lower detection limit which was obtained by previously reported methods (Table 1 ).

The reproducibility of MIP ECL sensor was evaluated by using five MIP electrodes to test ESM at equal concentrations, and RSD (n = 5) was 2.3%. And the repeatability of sensor were evaluated by using an electrode modified five times respectively, and RSD (n = 5) for ESM measurement was 1.5%. All these results indicated that the proposed ECL sensor for ESM determination performed good reproducibility and repeatability. At the same time, the storage stability of the proposed sensor was also investigated by monitoring of the ECL signal changes. After two weeks the response of the MIP ECL sensor for ESM decreased 12.5%.

The MIP sensor performs good selectivity and specificity based on the binding sites for recognition of template molecule. To further investigate the selectivity of the proposed MIP ECL sensor, contrast experiments were performed. The sensors were incubated with different interfering substances under the same experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 , compared with 1.00 × 10−6 mol/L ESM, these interfering substances (each has a concentration of 1.00 × 10−5 mol/L) including dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA), urea, uric acid (UA), glucose (GLC), glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Hg2+ and Pb2+ did not exhibit any obvious signal decrease. It indicated these ions and organics had no influence on ESM detection using MIP ECL sensor. Therefore, this ECL MIP sensor performed good selectivity for ESM.

To further evaluate the viability of the detection method, recovery experiments were carried out. The ESM with three different concentrations was respectively added into the blank rat plasma and well mixed, and then methanol was also added to mixture to precipitate protein. The supernatant was blown dry with nitrogen, and then was diluted with 0.1 mol/L PBS (pH 7.4) buffer. The rat urine samples was diluted with 0.1 mol/L PBS (pH 7.4) buffer, and into which the ESM with three different concentrations was added. The results obtained by standard addition method were shown in Table 2 . It showed that the recovery ranged from 97.8% to 106.4%, and the RSD was lower than 6.3%. Thus it demonstrated that the proposed method was reliable and had the potential for detection of ESM in biological samples.

A novel ECL MIP sensing platform based on Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs for ESM detection have beeen proposed. This new hybrid material of Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs profiting from the good electrical conductivity of N-GQDs, exhibited excellent ECL properties. In addition, PDA molecular imprinting technique was applied to increase selectivity of sensor. The detection limit of ESM is 3.00 × 10−7 mol/L, and the recovery in blank plasma or urine was reasonable. Thus, such efficient and simple molecularly imprinted on Ru(pby)3 2+@N-GQDs/Nafion system may be a good model for fabrication drug sensor, and has a potential application to assay ESM in the biological samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ethosuximide alters intrathalamic and thalamocortical synchronizing mechanisms: a possible explanation of its antiabsence effect

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. pPellegrini, R. C. Dossi, F. D. Pos, M. Ermani, L. Zanotto and G.
